title: Weather Hood
desc: The upper log station needs its hood repaired before the next ash gust turns the sight records into sludge.

steps.text:
The highest log station at Oipont's Lookout is little more than a desk, a rack of tablets, and a weather hood stubbornly pretending that counts as shelter. On calm days it is enough. Today the hood's side flap has torn loose at one corner and the morning air is already carrying ash grit fine enough to ruin a whole shift's notes if someone leaves it unchallenged.

The log keeper tucks her tablet under one arm and points to the frame. "Retie the weather flap if you want the canvas," she says. "Or sort the dry tablets and wax boards into the covered rack if you prefer the shelves. I would rather not record a hostile column on paper that dissolves halfway through the sentence."

options.label: Retie the torn weather flap on the log hood
Sort the dry tablets and wax boards into the rack

steps.text:
The flap is a heavy treated cloth meant to keep out ash, side gusts, and the sort of sideways moisture that occasionally visits the upper platforms without anyone inviting it. You draw the torn corner back into the frame, stitch in a fresh tie cord, and pull the whole side panel tight enough that it stops snapping and starts shielding. Once corrected, the hood recovers its modest dignity as an actual station instead of an elaborate suggestion.

The keeper stands inside the repaired shelter and watches the next ash stream pass harmlessly along the outside seam. That seems to settle the matter. "Good. We can return to mistrusting the distance instead of the furniture."

options.label: Tie off the last cord and leave the hood holding firm

steps.text:
The record rack holds dry clay tablets for formal entries and wax boards for quick notes that may later become formal entries if the horizon behaves badly enough. Right now the stacks have mingled, and the outer shelf is dusty enough to turn every fresh tablet into a gray thumbprint with official ambitions. You sort the boards by use, brush the rack clean, and return the whole station to a state where one can record incoming danger without first excavating writing supplies.

When the keeper slides a clean tablet into place and the rack gives it back without grit, she nods once. "Better. Observation is hard enough before bad shelving begins offering commentary."

options.label: Set the brush aside and leave the rack ready for reports
